county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Created 1914 from Missoula County. Western Montana mountain county along the Clark Fork River.
county_metadata.research_tips: Mineral County Clerk and Recorder in Superior has records from 1914. The Northern Pacific Railroad ran through the Clark Fork River valley, and railroad employment records at MHS and the Minnesota Historical Society identify workers. The Great Fire of 1910 devastated much of Mineral County's forests — fire survivor accounts and Forest Service records at NARA Seattle document the disaster and its impact on local families. Timber industry records (Anaconda Copper Mining Company timber operations) at MHS identify loggers and mill workers. Check Missoula County for pre-1914 records. The Mineral County Museum in Superior has local family files. St. Regis, Saltese, and other small communities along the railroad have distinct settlement histories. The Mullan Road (1860s military road) crossed the county; road construction records at NARA may name early residents.

source_profiles.coverage_notes: Grantor/grantee deed books from 1914 to present. County Clerk and Recorder maintains index books and recorded instruments.
Marriage licenses and certificates from 1914. County Clerk and Recorder issued licenses; state registration from 1943.

Civil and criminal case files, probate, guardianship, adoption, naturalization, and name changes from 1914.
Microfilmed county records including deeds, marriages, and probate. Browse-only image collections from Mineral County Clerk and Recorder's office.
Federal population schedules for Mineral County. Montana Territory enumerated from 1870. Agricultural and manufacturing schedules provide additional family details.
Historical newspapers from Mineral County communities. Montana Historical Society Newspaper Archives and Chronicling America have digitized Montana papers.

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Created 1865 as one of the original nine Montana Territory counties. Home to the University of Montana (1893) and the US Forest Service Region 1 headquarters.
county_metadata.research_tips: Missoula County Clerk and Recorder holds deed records from 1865. The University of Montana's Mansfield Library Archives and Special Collections has extensive Montana family papers, lumber industry records, and Forest Service historical files. Missoula was a trading post and milling center from the 1860s. The Historical Museum at Fort Missoula documents the military post (1877+) including the African American 25th Infantry Regiment. The US Forest Service Region 1 headquarters (est. 1908) records at NARA Seattle document rangers and their families across western Montana. Hellgate Village (1860) preceded Missoula; early settlement records at MHS. The Missoula Genealogy Society has active research collections. The Missoulian newspaper archives (1873+) are on microfilm at the public library and MHS.

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Created 1911 from parts of Fergus, Meagher, and Yellowstone counties. Named for the Musselshell River.
county_metadata.research_tips: Musselshell County Clerk and Recorder in Roundup has records from 1911. Roundup was a coal mining and cattle town — the Roundup Coal Mining Company and Republican Coal Company employment records at MHS identify miners, many of whom were Eastern European immigrants. The Musselshell Valley Historical Museum in Roundup has family files and photographs. The Milwaukee Road railroad came through the county bringing homesteaders. Check Fergus, Meagher, and Yellowstone counties for pre-1911 records. The Bull Mountains coal mining camps (Klein, Carpenter Creek) had distinct immigrant communities. BLM's GLO records document homestead entries. The Roundup Record-Tribune newspaper archives are on microfilm at MHS. Ranching predated both mining and farming; livestock brand records at the Montana Department of Livestock.

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Created 1887 from Gallatin County. Named for Yellowstone National Park. Livingston is the Northern Pacific gateway to Yellowstone.
county_metadata.research_tips: Park County Clerk and Recorder in Livingston has deed records from 1887. Livingston was founded in 1882 as the Northern Pacific Railroad's gateway to Yellowstone National Park — railroad shop and division point employment records at MHS and the Minnesota Historical Society identify workers. Yellowstone National Park records (1872+) at NARA document rangers, Army administrators, hotel employees, and concessionaires. The Livingston Depot Center and Park County Museum have local family files. Emigrant Gulch gold mining camp (1864) records at MHS predate the county. The Shields Valley and Paradise Valley ranching communities have distinct histories. Park County attracted dude ranch operators from the 1920s; guest ranch records may survive in private collections and at MHS.

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Created 1925 from Fergus County. Montana's least-populated county. Named for oil and gas deposits.
county_metadata.research_tips: Petroleum County Clerk and Recorder in Winnett has records from 1925. Petroleum County is Montana's least-populated county (consistently under 500 residents). The county was created when the Cat Creek oil field (discovered 1920) brought brief prosperity. Check Fergus County for pre-1925 records. Montana Oil and Gas Conservation Division records document well operators and landowners. The Petroleum County Museum in Winnett has local family files. Ranching families who predated the oil boom are documented in livestock brand books at the Montana Department of Livestock. BLM's GLO records document homestead entries. The extreme small population means county commissioner meeting minutes, school censuses, and the Winnett Times newspaper are particularly valuable — nearly every family appears in these records.

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Created 1915 from Blaine and Valley counties. Home of the Charles M. Russell National Wildlife Refuge.
county_metadata.research_tips: Phillips County Clerk and Recorder in Malta has records from 1915. Phillips County was homesteaded during the 1909-1918 boom — many claims along the Milk River valley and bench lands. The Phillips County Museum in Malta has local family files and homestead-era photographs. Kid Curry (Harvey Logan) and the Wild Bunch had hideouts in the Missouri River Breaks; outlaw-era records and newspaper accounts at MHS. Check Blaine and Valley counties for pre-1915 records. The Great Northern Railway Hi-Line brought settlers; railroad land records at the Minnesota Historical Society and MHS. Fort Peoples (CCC camp) records during the 1930s at NARA. Ranching families in the Missouri Breaks are documented in livestock brand books. Malta newspaper archives on microfilm at MHS.

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Created 1919 from Teton and Chouteau counties. Named for the Pondera River (tributary of the Marias).
county_metadata.research_tips: Pondera County Clerk and Recorder in Conrad has records from 1919. Conrad was a Great Northern Railway town named for William G. Conrad, early Montana banker and merchant. The Pondera County Museum in Conrad holds homesteading-era family records. Dupuyer and Heart Butte communities have distinct settlement histories — Heart Butte is within the Blackfeet Reservation. Check Teton and Chouteau counties for pre-1919 records. The Valier Irrigation Project (1905+) brought homesteaders to irrigated farmland; Bureau of Reclamation records at NARA Denver document water rights holders. BLM's GLO records document original homestead patents. Scandinavian and German-Russian immigrant families are prominent in county records. The Pondera County newspaper archives are on microfilm at MHS.

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Created 1919 from Custer County. Remote southeastern Montana cattle country.
county_metadata.research_tips: Powder River County Clerk and Recorder in Broadus holds records from 1919. Powder River County is quintessential open-range cattle country — the Powder River valley was a major cattle corridor from Texas trail drives in the 1880s. Montana Stockgrowers Association records at MHS identify early ranchers. Check Custer County for pre-1919 records. The Powder River Historical Museum in Broadus has ranching family files. Homesteaders arrived during the 1909-1918 boom, but most claims failed during drought; BLM's GLO records document these entries. Military campaigns against the Lakota and Northern Cheyenne in the 1870s crossed the Powder River country — campaign records at NARA. Livestock brand books at the Montana Department of Livestock trace ranching families across generations. The Broadus newspaper archives are on microfilm at MHS.

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Created 1901 from Deer Lodge County. Named for the Powell mining district. Deer Lodge (city) is the county seat — distinct from Deer Lodge County.
county_metadata.research_tips: Powell County Clerk and Recorder in Deer Lodge (city) has records from 1901. Montana State Prison has been in Deer Lodge since 1871 — prison records at MHS include inmate registers, commitment papers, and prisoner correspondence from across the state. The Powell County Museum in Deer Lodge and the Old Montana Prison museum have local history collections. The Grant-Kohrs Ranch National Historic Site preserves one of Montana's great cattle ranch records; ranch ledgers and correspondence at the site and NARA document employees and suppliers. Check Deer Lodge County for pre-1901 records. Gold Creek (site of Montana's first gold discovery, 1852) is in Powell County; early mining district records at MHS. The Northern Pacific Railroad ran through the county.

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Created 1915 from parts of Custer, Dawson, and Fallon counties.
county_metadata.research_tips: Prairie County Clerk and Recorder in Terry has records from 1915. Prairie County is named for its grassland terrain and is sparsely populated. The Prairie County Museum in Terry has homestead-era family photographs and records. Homesteaders arrived during the 1909-1918 boom along the Northern Pacific Railroad; many claims were abandoned during drought. Check Custer, Dawson, and Fallon counties for pre-1915 records. BLM's GLO records document all federal land patents. The Terry Tribune newspaper archives are on microfilm at MHS. The Yellowstone River valley communities along the railroad have distinct settlement histories from the bench-land homesteaders. Ranching families are documented in livestock brand books at the Montana Department of Livestock. Evelyn Cameron, the English photographer, documented life near Terry in the 1890s-1920s; her photo collection at the MHS is a unique genealogical resource.
